input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is an incubator CPS consisting of a styrofoam insulated container, a heating element (heat‑bed), a circulation fan, three temperature sensors (two inside the chamber, one external), all orchestrated by a Raspberry Pi controller; its purpose is to reach and maintain a target temperature regardless of interior content, making it a representative low‑complexity CPS for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides simulation‑based prediction of the incubator’s thermal behaviour, calibration algorithms that continuously adjust the cyber‑physical models using sensor data, monitoring services that check safety and optimality of temperature control, and optimisation services that can suggest or enact control actions; these services support engineers and operators by enabling model‑based analysis, fault detection, and supervisory control.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on the Raspberry Pi hardware platform as the physical controller, temperature sensors for data acquisition, and a publicly available GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example‑incubator) that supplies the hardware schematics, software source code, and model definitions used by the Digital Twin; calibration and monitoring algorithms constitute the core software enablers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin follows a modular pipeline architecture: sensor data from the Physical Twin is ingested, calibration algorithms update the cyber‑physical system and environment models with the best‑estimate parameters, the refreshed models feed monitoring components that assess safety and optimality, and upon detection of violations a decision component (human operator or automated algorithm) triggers corrective actions on the Physical Twin. This reflects coordinated orchestration of data acquisition, model calibration, monitoring, and actuation as described in Document 1.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT integrates data collected from the physical twin via sensors over time and a set of computational models that capture both cyber and physical aspects of the incubator and its environment. Two simulation models of the incubator temperature are provided: a simple model with two free parameters and a more detailed model with four free parameters that can capture the thermal inertia of the heat‑bed. Calibration algorithms continuously adjust model parameters using the latest sensor data to produce the best estimate of the system state. The software stack for communication and data handling is open‑source and links the controller, sensor data streams, and the simulation models within the DT framework.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by comparing two temperature prediction models: the four‑parameter model, which captures the heat‑bed’s thermal inertia, demonstrated higher predictive accuracy in experimental trials and was therefore selected for further DT development. Validation is performed through empirical experiments that measure the incubator temperature and compare it to model outputs. Calibration algorithms are used to update model parameters based on observed data, providing a mechanism for error correction. The documents do not detail uncertainty quantification, error detection methods, or broader quality‑assurance processes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Physical Twin (PT) provides temperature data from three sensors – two sensors inside the incubator box and one sensor outside – to the Digital Twin (DT). The data consist of numeric temperature measurements collected over time. The documents state that data are collected through sensors and fed into the DT for simulation and monitoring, but they do not specify the communication protocol, transmission frequency, event‑triggering conditions, data‑quality assurance methods, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DT can issue control actions back to the PT to regulate the incubator temperature. Commands are directed to the heatbed (heating element) and the fan (air distribution). The documents mention that the DT may make changes to the physical twin, but they do not describe the command format, validation or authentication mechanisms, feedback loops, conflict‑resolution strategies, or confirmation of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT architecture includes a cyber‑physical link that enables data acquisition from sensors and command transmission to actuators, but the documents do not provide details on the underlying network infrastructure such as specific communication protocols, network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The documents describe the DT as a framework that integrates models, algorithms, and data, yet they give no information about the hosting environment (cloud, edge, or on‑premise), computational or storage resources, scalability approaches, deployment models, or availability and reliability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin continuously runs calibration algorithms that ingest sensor data from the physical twin, update the cyber‑physical models, and execute simulations to predict current behavior. Monitoring components compare simulated and measured states to detect safety or performance violations. When a violation is detected, the DT generates an alert or recommendation that is presented to a human operator (or higher‑level algorithm) who can decide to modify the physical twin’s control actions. Thus, the DT provides real‑time insight into system health, predictive warnings, and decision support for corrective actions.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin framework described integrates monitoring algorithms that continuously evaluate sensor data collected from the Physical Twin to detect safety violations. When a breach of predefined safety constraints is identified, the DT triggers alerts and a decision loop that may involve human operators or autonomous controllers to enact corrective actions on the Physical Twin. This safety‑centric monitoring is explicitly mentioned as a core part of the DT architecture, aiming to keep the system operating within safe limits and to enable predictive safety checks.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and detailed. It accurately lists the components: 'styrofoam insulated container', 'heating element (heat-bed)', 'circulation fan', and 'three temperature sensors'. The text confirms these details: 'The plant of the Incubator consists of... A styrofoam box...', 'A heatbed...', 'A fan...', and 'Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside... One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside'. It correctly identifies the 'Raspberry Pi controller' and the system's purpose to 'reach and maintain a target temperature regardless of interior content', which matches the source: 'main goal... is to reach a certain temperature within a box and regulate it regardless of the content inside'.

The extraction accurately reflects the general services described for the Digital Twin in the text. It mentions 'simulation-based prediction' which aligns with 'ability to run simulations that reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT'. It cites 'calibration algorithms' which matches the source stating 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models'. It includes 'monitoring services' corresponding to 'inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally'. The mention of 'optimisation services' aligns with the text listing 'optimisers' as algorithms used. The extraction is specific to the services discussed in the source.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Raspberry Pi hardware platform' ('A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B was selected to implement the controller') and the 'publicly available GitHub repository' ('The publicly available repository [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator] contains the up-to-date hardware and software specifications'). It mentions 'temperature sensors for data acquisition' and 'calibration and monitoring algorithms', which are supported by the text regarding the DT's use of 'Data', 'Models', and 'Algorithms'. The description is specific and well-grounded in the provided sources.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_5_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container



Temperature





Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.
